narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path despite the challenges many face, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admission system uses a predictive model trained on data from thousands of students, looking at factors like grades, test scores, family background, and study plans to forecast the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. For you, the model estimated a 52% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for denial—meaning it flagged your profile as higher risk compared to applicants predicted to pass. The biggest reason for this prediction was your LSAT score of 21, which is well below the typical applicant's score of around 37 and strongly pushed the model toward expecting failure. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 was close to the average of 3.21, helping pull the prediction slightly toward success. Your strong Year 1 school decile ranking of 8—better than the average of about 6—also worked in your favor, nudging the odds toward passing. Your Year 3 decile of 5 was right around the typical 5.5, offering a modest positive influence too. Your lower-middle family income bracket added a small push toward the failure prediction, while your plan to study full-time—shared by over 90% of students—provided a tiny boost in the right direction. Overall, your LSAT stood out as the most influential factor driving the rejection, outweighing your solid academic deciles and near-average GPA when compared to typical successful applicants who often have much higher test scores. This doesn't define your potential; many students in similar circumstances have succeeded by targeting their weaker areas. I encourage you to retake the LSAT to boost that score, strengthen your GPA if possible through additional coursework, and consider resources tailored for Black women in law like targeted prep programs or mentorship networks. With focused effort on these, you can reshape your profile and reapply stronger—your journey is far from over. Keep pushing forward; you've already come this far.
